content.text: An MCP Client is the protocol state machine that manages one connection to one MCP server. In the Python SDK, it is
— an async context manager that handles the initialize handshake, message serialization, request ID tracking, and clean shutdown. You do not implement it; you use it.

attrs.alt: MCP client lifecycle and state machine: CONNECTING → INITIALIZING → READY → CLOSING → CLOSED

content.text: Use the async context manager pattern for every client connection. It guarantees the server process is started before use and terminated on exit — even if an exception occurs mid-session.
For local servers (subprocess over stdin/stdout), combine

import asyncio
from mcp import ClientSession, StdioServerParameters
from mcp.client.stdio import stdio_client

async def main():
    # StdioServerParameters defines what subprocess to start
    params = StdioServerParameters(
        command="python",
        args=["weather_server.py"],
        env=None,          # inherits the current process environment
    )

    # stdio_client starts the subprocess and opens stdin/stdout pipes
    async with stdio_client(params) as (read, write):
        # ClientSession manages the JSON-RPC state machine
        async with ClientSession(read, write) as session:
            # initialize() sends the handshake and waits for capabilities
            await session.initialize()

            # Now in READY state — can list and call tools
            tools = await session.list_tools()
            print(f"Connected: {len(tools.tools)} tools")
            for tool in tools.tools:
                print(f"  {tool.name}: {tool.description}")

asyncio.run(main())
blocks correspond to two layers: the transport (subprocess I/O) and the protocol session. Exiting the inner block sends a close notification; exiting the outer block terminates the subprocess.
once the session is initialized. The return value is a

from mcp import ClientSession, StdioServerParameters
from mcp.client.stdio import stdio_client
from mcp.types import CallToolResult
import asyncio

async def call_weather(city: str) -> str:
    params = StdioServerParameters(command="python", args=["weather_server.py"])
    async with stdio_client(params) as (read, write):
        async with ClientSession(read, write) as session:
            await session.initialize()

            result: CallToolResult = await session.call_tool(
                name="get_weather",
                arguments={"city": city},
            )

            # result.isError is True if the server returned an error response
            if result.isError:
                error_text = "\n".join(
                    c.text for c in result.content if hasattr(c, "text")
                )
                raise RuntimeError(f"Tool error: {error_text}")

            # result.content is a list of content blocks (TextContent, ImageContent, etc.)
            return "\n".join(c.text for c in result.content if hasattr(c, "text"))

print(asyncio.run(call_weather("London")))
before using the content. An error result is not a Python exception — the server returned an application-level failure and the session is still healthy.
If the server exposes Resources, list them with

from mcp import ClientSession, StdioServerParameters
from mcp.client.stdio import stdio_client
import asyncio

async def read_project_readme() -> str:
    params = StdioServerParameters(command="python", args=["fs_server.py"])
    async with stdio_client(params) as (read, write):
        async with ClientSession(read, write) as session:
            await session.initialize()

            # List available resources
            resources_result = await session.list_resources()
            for resource in resources_result.resources:
                print(f"  {resource.uri} ({resource.mimeType})")

            # Read a specific resource by URI
            content = await session.read_resource("file:///README.md")
            # content.contents is a list of text or blob content items
            return "\n".join(
                c.text for c in content.contents if hasattr(c, "text")
            )
Connecting to a Remote Server (HTTP + SSE)
For servers hosted on a remote machine, use
API is identical — only the transport changes:

# HTTP+SSE transport: connect to a remote MCP server
from mcp import ClientSession
from mcp.client.sse import sse_client
import asyncio, os

async def connect_remote(url: str):
    api_key = os.environ["MCP_API_KEY"]
    # sse_client establishes an HTTP connection and opens an SSE stream
    async with sse_client(url, headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {api_key}"}) as (read, write):
        async with ClientSession(read, write) as session:
            await session.initialize()
            tools = await session.list_tools()
            print(f"Remote server: {len(tools.tools)} tools")

asyncio.run(connect_remote("https://mcp.example.com/sse"))

An agent that uses tools from several servers opens one ClientSession per server, aggregates their tool lists (with name prefixes to avoid collisions), and dispatches each tool call to the correct session:

import asyncio
import anthropic
from mcp import ClientSession, StdioServerParameters
from mcp.client.stdio import stdio_client

claude = anthropic.Anthropic()

async def run_multi_server_agent(user_message: str) -> str:
    github_params = StdioServerParameters(command="npx", args=["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-github"])
    slack_params  = StdioServerParameters(command="python", args=["slack_server.py"])

    async with stdio_client(github_params) as (r1, w1), \
               stdio_client(slack_params)  as (r2, w2):
        async with ClientSession(r1, w1) as github, \
                   ClientSession(r2, w2) as slack:
            await github.initialize()
            await slack.initialize()

            # Aggregate tools from both, prefix names to keep them unique
            github_tools_raw = (await github.list_tools()).tools
            slack_tools_raw  = (await slack.list_tools()).tools

            tools = (
                [{"name": f"github__{t.name}", "description": t.description or "",
                  "input_schema": t.inputSchema} for t in github_tools_raw]
                + [{"name": f"slack__{t.name}", "description": t.description or "",
                    "input_schema": t.inputSchema} for t in slack_tools_raw]
            )

            github_names = {t.name for t in github_tools_raw}
            slack_names  = {t.name for t in slack_tools_raw}

            messages = [{"role": "user", "content": user_message}]
            while True:
                resp = claude.messages.create(
                    model="claude-sonnet-4-6", max_tokens=2048,
                    tools=tools, messages=messages,
                )
                if resp.stop_reason == "end_turn":
                    return next(b.text for b in resp.content if hasattr(b, "text"))
                if resp.stop_reason != "tool_use":
                    break

                tool_results = []
                for block in resp.content:
                    if block.type != "tool_use":
                        continue
                    if block.name.startswith("github__"):
                        actual = block.name[len("github__"):]
                        r = await github.call_tool(actual, block.input)
                    elif block.name.startswith("slack__"):
                        actual = block.name[len("slack__"):]
                        r = await slack.call_tool(actual, block.input)
                    else:
                        continue
                    text = "\n".join(c.text for c in r.content if hasattr(c, "text"))
                    tool_results.append({"type": "tool_result",
                                         "tool_use_id": block.id, "content": text})

                messages.append({"role": "assistant", "content": resp.content})
                messages.append({"role": "user", "content": tool_results})
Open all connections in a single
block to keep the code flat and ensure all servers are cleaned up together.
Transport errors (server crash, process killed) raise Python exceptions from the SDK. Retry by re-opening the context managers — the server restarts from scratch:
(exceptions): reconnect by re-entering the context managers.
): return the error text to the model; the session remains usable.
— a bad tool argument will fail every time. Only retry on transport-level failures.
Test client code without starting real servers. Mock
and inject it into the code under test:

import asyncio, pytest
from unittest.mock import AsyncMock, MagicMock
from mcp import types

# Patch the session so no subprocess is started
async def test_list_tools_schema():
    session = AsyncMock()
    session.list_tools.return_value = MagicMock(tools=[
        MagicMock(name="get_weather", description="Get weather.",
                  inputSchema={"type": "object", "properties": {
                      "city": {"type": "string"}}, "required": ["city"]}),
    ])
    result = await session.list_tools()
    tool = result.tools[0]
    assert tool.name == "get_weather"
    assert "city" in tool.inputSchema["properties"]

async def test_call_tool_success():
    session = AsyncMock()
    session.call_tool.return_value = MagicMock(
        content=[MagicMock(text="London: 15°C, cloudy", type="text")],
        isError=False,
    )
    result = await session.call_tool("get_weather", {"city": "London"})
    assert not result.isError
    assert "London" in result.content[0].text

async def test_call_tool_error_does_not_raise():
    session = AsyncMock()
    session.call_tool.return_value = MagicMock(
        content=[MagicMock(text="Error: city not found", type="text")],
        isError=True,
    )
    result = await session.call_tool("get_weather", {"city": "INVALID"})
    assert result.isError
    assert "not found" in result.content[0].text
Parallel Tool Calls Within One Session

A single ClientSession handles multiple concurrent tool calls — JSON-RPC request IDs disambiguate which response belongs to which request. Use asyncio.gather to issue calls in parallel and collect all results before proceeding:

import asyncio
from mcp import ClientSession

async def call_parallel_tools(
    session: ClientSession,
    calls: list[tuple[str, dict]],
) -> list[str]:
    """Issue N tool calls to one server concurrently.
    JSON-RPC IDs disambiguate responses; the server handles all calls in parallel.
    """
    results = await asyncio.gather(
        *[session.call_tool(name, args) for name, args in calls],
        return_exceptions=True,
    )
    outputs = []
    for i, result in enumerate(results):
        if isinstance(result, Exception):
            outputs.append(f"Error in {calls[i][0]}: {result}")
        elif result.isError:
            outputs.append("\n".join(c.text for c in result.content if hasattr(c, "text")))
        else:
            outputs.append("\n".join(c.text for c in result.content if hasattr(c, "text")))
    return outputs

# Example: fetch PR list and issue list simultaneously
# results = await call_parallel_tools(github_session, [
#     ("list_pull_requests", {"owner": "org", "repo": "api"}),
#     ("list_issues",        {"owner": "org", "repo": "api", "state": "open"}),
# ])
This is most valuable when an agent turn emits several independent tool_use blocks. Dispatching them in parallel reduces wall-clock time proportional to the slowest call, not the sum of all calls.

The initialize() result includes a capabilities object that describes which MCP primitives the server supports. Check capabilities before calling list_tools() or list_resources() — calling a method the server doesn't support raises a protocol error:

from mcp import ClientSession, StdioServerParameters
from mcp.client.stdio import stdio_client
import asyncio

async def inspect_server_capabilities(command: list[str]):
    params = StdioServerParameters(command=command[0], args=command[1:])
    async with stdio_client(params) as (read, write):
        async with ClientSession(read, write) as session:
            init_result = await session.initialize()

            caps = init_result.capabilities
            print("Protocol version:", init_result.serverInfo.version)
            print("Has tools:    ", caps.tools is not None)
            print("Has resources:", caps.resources is not None)
            print("Has prompts:  ", caps.prompts is not None)
            print("Has sampling: ", caps.sampling is not None)

            # Only call list_tools if the server advertised tool support
            if caps.tools:
                tools = await session.list_tools()
                print(f"Tools ({len(tools.tools)}):")
                for tool in tools.tools:
                    print(f"  {tool.name}")

            if caps.resources:
                resources = await session.list_resources()
                print(f"Resources ({len(resources.resources)}):")
                for r in resources.resources:
                    print(f"  {r.uri}")

asyncio.run(inspect_server_capabilities(['python', 'my_server.py']))
: server can initiate model sampling (advanced use case)
Most servers implement tools only. Checking capabilities lets your client code adapt to heterogeneous servers without hard-coding assumptions about what each one supports.
— an async context manager that handles the full protocol lifecycle.
async with stdio_client(...) as (read, write): async with ClientSession(read, write) as session:
Prefix tool names when aggregating from multiple servers to prevent name collisions.
Retry transport errors by re-opening context managers; don't retry application errors.
